---
title: "Synthetic Control Sources"
---

# Scope

This note is a source map for synthetic control method reading. It is organized around the kinds of sources most worth taking notes on: surveys and textbooks, seminal articles, lecture materials, and software manuals [@abadie2021using; @cunningham2018synthetic; @mixtape-causal-inference-3; @mixtape-synthetic-control-and-clustering; @synth-package; @tidysynth-package; @augsynth-package; @synthdid-package; @scpi-package; @gsynth-package].

# Start Here

If you want a compact starting stack, I would begin with these three:

1. Abadie (2021) for the best high-level methodological overview [@abadie2021using].
2. Cunningham's *Causal Inference: The Mixtape* chapter plus Mixtape Sessions slides for intuition and pedagogy ([chapter note](mixtape-synthetic-control-notes.qmd); [sessions note](mixtape-sessions-synthetic-control-notes.qmd)) [@cunningham2018synthetic; @mixtape-sessions-synthtex].
3. The `Synth` package paper and manual for implementation details ([note](synth-package-notes.qmd)) [@hainmueller2011synth; @synth-package].

That combination gives you design logic, identification intuition, and the original software workflow.

# Textbooks And Broad Overviews

## Abadie (2021), *Using Synthetic Controls: Feasibility, Data Requirements, and Methodological Aspects*

This is the best single overview if the goal is to understand when SCM is credible and when it is not ([note](abadie-synthetic-controls-notes.qmd)) [@abadie2021using].

Why it is worth notes:

- it is a mature retrospective by one of the method's originators
- it focuses on feasibility and failure conditions, not just estimation mechanics
- it is a strong bridge from textbook intuition to actual applied design choices

## Cunningham (2018), *Causal Inference: The Mixtape*, chapter on synthetic control

This is the best textbook-style source for intuition-first notes ([note](mixtape-synthetic-control-notes.qmd)) [@cunningham2018synthetic].

Why it is worth notes:

- it is easier to read quickly than the original articles
- it gives the method in causal-inference language rather than only optimization language
- it pairs naturally with the Mixtape Sessions materials

# Seminal Articles

## Abadie and Gardeazabal (2003)

This is the origin paper for the method and the Basque Country case study [@abadie2003economic].

Why it is worth notes:

- first clear comparative-case-study application
- establishes the core SCM logic
- useful for understanding what the method was trying to solve originally

## Abadie, Diamond, and Hainmueller (2010)

This is the canonical JASA article on California Proposition 99 [@abadie2010synthetic].

Why it is worth notes:

- formalizes the now-standard SCM setup
- the smoking example is still the default teaching application
- many software examples are built around this paper

## Abadie, Diamond, and Hainmueller (2015)

This paper is especially useful if you care about comparative case studies and the logic of bridging qualitative and quantitative work [@abadie2015comparative].

Why it is worth notes:

- emphasizes SCM as a design for small-sample comparative research
- uses German reunification as a major application
- helps clarify how SCM differs from ordinary regression-based comparisons

# Modern Extensions Worth A Separate Round Of Notes

## Ben-Michael, Feller, and Rothstein (2021)

This is the key augmented SCM paper [@benmichael2021augmented].

Why it is worth notes:

- directly addresses imperfect pre-treatment fit
- introduces outcome-model bias correction in a way that is practically important
- underpins the `augsynth` workflow

## Arkhangelsky et al. (2021)

This is the synthetic DiD paper [@arkhangelsky2021synthetic].

Why it is worth notes:

- shows how SCM-style weighting and DiD-style differencing can be combined
- especially useful for panels with more than one treated unit
- important if you want to understand where SCM and DiD now overlap

## Xu (2017)

This is the generalized synthetic control paper [@xu2017generalized].

Why it is worth notes:

- extends SCM using interactive fixed effects
- handles multiple treated units and more complex treatment timing
- useful if you want to connect SCM to matrix-completion and factor-model approaches

# Lecture Notes And Slide Decks

## Mixtape Sessions: Causal Inference 3

Scott Cunningham's `Causal-Inference-3` repository is one of the best open teaching resources for SCM ([note](mixtape-sessions-synthetic-control-notes.qmd)) [@mixtape-causal-inference-3; @mixtape-sessions-synthtex].

Why it is worth notes:

- long-form lecture treatment of original SCM and newer extensions
- combines theory, applications, and software
- especially good for note-taking on intuition and workflow

## Mixtape Sessions: Synthetic Control and Clustering

Alberto Abadie's Mixtape Sessions repository is a high-value source if you want notes closer to the frontier and closer to the original research perspective [@mixtape-synthetic-control-and-clustering].

Why it is worth notes:

- focuses on practical principles for empirical SCM work
- includes estimation and inference guidance
- good complement to the Cunningham materials

## Jens Hainmueller's Synthetic Control Methods Project Page

This is a compact hub tying together the major papers, software, and an explainer from one of the original developers [@hainmueller-scm-project].

Why it is worth notes:

- convenient source hub
- useful for finding the original papers and package links quickly
- good place to orient before drilling into specific papers

# Software Manuals And Implementation Sources

## `Synth`

The foundational implementation source is the `Synth` package paper and the package manual ([note](synth-package-notes.qmd)) [@hainmueller2011synth; @synth-package].

Take notes on:

- `dataprep`
- predictor specification
- donor weights and predictor weights
- path plots, gap plots, and placebo workflows

## `tidysynth`

Use `tidysynth` if you want a tidyverse-oriented implementation that is easier to teach and inspect step by step [@tidysynth-package].

Take notes on:

- pipeline-based data preparation
- inspectable balance, weights, and placebo outputs
- the `smoking` teaching dataset

## `augsynth`

Use `augsynth` when your notes need to cover imperfect fit and augmentation [@augsynth-package].

Take notes on:

- ridge augmentation
- simultaneous-adoption versus staggered-adoption workflows
- how augmentation changes the interpretation of weights and fit

## `synthdid`

Use `synthdid` if your notes need to compare SCM to modern panel estimators [@synthdid-vignette].

Take notes on:

- unit and time weighting
- the relationship to two-way fixed effects
- event-study style visualization and inference choices

## `scpi`

Use `scpi` if your notes need a serious treatment of uncertainty and prediction intervals [@scpi-package].

Take notes on:

- uncertainty quantification
- multiple treated units and staggered adoption
- how interval estimation changes applied interpretation

## `gsynth`

Use `gsynth` for notes on generalized SCM and interactive fixed effects [@gsynth-package; @gsynth-manual].

Take notes on:

- factor-model formulation
- handling unbalanced panels
- cross-validation for factor selection

# Suggested Note-Taking Order

If you want a sequence that compounds well, use:

1. `Causal Inference: The Mixtape` synthetic control chapter ([chapter note](mixtape-synthetic-control-notes.qmd)) [@cunningham2018synthetic].
2. Abadie and Gardeazabal (2003) [@abadie2003economic].
3. Abadie, Diamond, and Hainmueller (2010) [@abadie2010synthetic].
4. Abadie (2021) [@abadie2021using].
5. `Synth` package paper and manual [@hainmueller2011synth; @synth-package].
6. Mixtape Sessions lecture slides and SCM workshop repositories ([Mixtape Sessions note](mixtape-sessions-synthetic-control-notes.qmd)) [@mixtape-sessions-synthtex; @mixtape-synthetic-control-and-clustering].
7. Ben-Michael, Feller, and Rothstein (2021), Arkhangelsky et al. (2021), and Xu (2017) for extensions [@benmichael2021augmented; @arkhangelsky2021synthetic; @xu2017generalized].
8. `augsynth`, `synthdid`, `scpi`, and `gsynth` package docs for implementation details [@augsynth-package; @synthdid-package; @scpi-package; @gsynth-package].

# Bottom Line

If I were deciding what to take notes on first, I would prioritize four source types:

- one survey: Abadie (2021) [@abadie2021using]
- one textbook treatment: Cunningham (2018) [@cunningham2018synthetic]
- two seminal papers: Abadie and Gardeazabal (2003) and Abadie, Diamond, and Hainmueller (2010) [@abadie2003economic; @abadie2010synthetic]
- the software manuals that match the estimators you actually plan to use, especially `Synth`, `augsynth`, `synthdid`, `scpi`, and `gsynth` [@synth-package; @augsynth-package; @synthdid-package; @scpi-package; @gsynth-package]

That gives you a clean path from original idea, to canonical design, to practical implementation, to modern extensions.
